Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories Directed At Defendant Budi Harjono And For Monetary Sanctions
Off Calendar Jan-11-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Attorney Albert Lee telephone number 415-215-6868 for Plaintiff/Moving Party.
No other appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF ALBERT LEE'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories Directed At Defendant Budi Harjono And For Monetary Sanctions - The Court adopts the recommendation of JPT Robert Kane, which is as follows:
This is an auto accident case in which Plaintiff claims that his car was rear ended because the defendant driver was distracted because he was on a cell phone or some other electronic wireless device. Plaintiff seeks to have the defendant by special interrogatory identify all cellular or other electronic wireless communication devises that were within his possession, custody or control on the date of the accident (December 15, 2012). Defendant objected that the requests were "burdensome, harassing, annoying, lack specificity in regard to the type of information actually sought; vague as to include information that is protected the attorney-client privilege and the work product doctrine and violates basic right to privacy." Defendant then responded by stating that he was "not in the immediate possession or control" of any cell phones or electronic devices at the time of the incident." Plaintiff seeks to challenge Defendant's response by getting information regarding all cell phones that he had custody or control over, including those of family members to the extent that he has such custody or control. Plaintiff claims that defendant has waived its right to object because of a late response. Serving discovery by mail on the individual defendants, prior to an appearance, even though done 10 days after service, even if authorized, is highly unusual. (See Mathews v. CCSF (1958) 158 Cal.App.2d 169, 173 [presumed need for actual service before appearance]; Code of Civ. Proc. 465, 1010 [after appearance papers may be served on counsel by mail].) As a result, I would not deem any of the objections as having been waived. With respect to the merits, these questions are relevant to the subject matter and at this stage of the proceedings no records are being sought. It does not appear that any of the objections have merit. These interrogatories do not request information that is burdensome, harassing, annoying, nor does it violate the attorney-client or work product privileges. The term possession, custody or control has been broadly interpreted, but to the extent that Defendant claims its lacks specificity, it will be interpreted to mean cell phones or electronic devices that include those of for family members for whom Defendant pays the bills. To the extent that this infringes on any privacy right, Plaintiff has no other way to get this information to refute Defendant's position.
Once this information is obtained from Defendant, it is not clear what Plaintiff will do with it, but to the extent that Plaintiff seeks to obtain phone or other records, Defendant has not waived any objections as to relevance or privacy or the right to protective orders or in camera hearings. No sanctions are awarded as Defendant disclosed that he did not have any such devices in his immediate possession and Plaintiff seeks to test this response. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Giselle Casey, CSR #8098. (302/EG)
